chapters.chapter: The Rise of Nationalism in Europe

topics.topic: Frederic Sorrieu and the Dream of Nations
topics.meaning: Frederic Sorrieu was a French artist who in 1848 prepared prints visualising a world made up of democratic and social Republics organised as nations.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In 1848 Frederic Sorrieu, a French artist, prepared a series of four prints visualising his dream of a world made up of 'democratic and social Republics'. The first print shows peoples of Europe and America, men and women of all ages and classes, marching and offering homage to the Statue of Liberty, who bears the torch of Enlightenment and the Charter of the Rights of Man. On the ground lie the shattered remains of the symbols of absolutist institutions. In Sorrieu's utopian vision the peoples of the world are grouped as distinct nations, identified through their flags and national costumes, led by the United States and Switzerland which were already nation-states. France, Germany, Austria and other peoples follow, while Christ, saints and angels gaze from the heavens symbolising fraternity among nations. The image captures the spirit of the nineteenth century when nationalism emerged as a force that reshaped Europe.
topics.easyExplanation: An artist named Sorrieu drew pictures in 1848 showing people of different countries marching together as free nations to celebrate liberty.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Frederic Sorrieu, a French artist, made four prints in 1848.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: He imagined a world of democratic and social Republics organised as nations.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Shattered symbols of absolutist rule lie on the ground.

topics.topic: The French Revolution and the Idea of the Nation
topics.meaning: The French Revolution of 1789 was the first clear expression of nationalism, transferring sovereignty from the monarchy to the French people.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The first clear expression of nationalism came with the French Revolution in 1789. Political and constitutional changes transferred sovereignty from the monarchy to a body of French citizens, proclaiming that the people would constitute the nation and shape its destiny. Ideas of la patrie (the fatherland) and le citoyen (the citizen) emphasised a united community enjoying equal rights under a constitution. A new French flag, the tricolour, replaced the royal standard; the Estates General was elected and renamed the National Assembly; new hymns were composed and martyrs commemorated. A centralised administrative system made uniform laws, internal customs duties were abolished, and a uniform system of weights and measures was adopted. The French language as spoken in Paris became the common national language. French revolutionaries also declared it their mission to help other peoples of Europe become nations.
topics.easyExplanation: In 1789 the French Revolution gave power to the people instead of the king, and created a common French identity with shared symbols, laws and language.

topics.topic: Napoleon and the Civil Code of 1804
topics.meaning: Napoleon Bonaparte introduced reforms including the Civil Code of 1804 that abolished privileges based on birth and spread revolutionary principles across Europe.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Although Napoleon destroyed democracy in France, he incorporated revolutionary principles in the administration to make the system more rational and efficient. The Civil Code of 1804, known as the Napoleonic Code, did away with all privileges based on birth, established equality before the law and secured the right to property. Napoleon carried this code to the regions under French control, including the Dutch Republic, Switzerland, Italy and Germany. He simplified administrative divisions, abolished the feudal system, freed peasants from serfdom and manorial dues, and removed guild restrictions in towns. Transport and communication systems were improved, and peasants, artisans, workers and businessmen enjoyed new freedom. Uniform laws, standardised weights and measures, and a common national currency facilitated movement and exchange. Initially many welcomed the French armies, but enthusiasm turned to hostility as increased taxation, censorship and forced conscription outweighed the benefits.
topics.easyExplanation: Napoleon made new laws like the Civil Code of 1804 that gave everyone equality before the law and ended feudal privileges, spreading these ideas to lands France controlled.

topics.topic: Liberal Nationalism and the Middle Classes
topics.meaning: Liberal nationalism in the early nineteenth century combined demands for individual freedom, equality before law, constitutional government and free markets, led mainly by the educated middle classes.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In the first half of the nineteenth century nationalism in Europe was closely allied to the ideology of liberalism. For the new middle classes, liberalism stood for individual freedom and equality of all before the law. Politically it emphasised government by consent, the end of autocracy and clerical privileges, a constitution and representative government through parliament. However, the right to vote was restricted to property-owning men, while women and non-propertied men were excluded. In the economic sphere liberalism stood for the freedom of markets and the abolition of state-imposed restrictions on the movement of goods and capital. In 1834 a customs union or zollverein was formed at Prussia's initiative and joined by most German states, abolishing tariff barriers and reducing the number of currencies. This shows how economic demands and national unity were linked in the liberal movement.
topics.easyExplanation: Liberalism meant freedom and equality before the law, a constitution and elected parliament, plus free trade; it was supported mainly by the educated middle classes.

topics.topic: The Congress of Vienna and Conservatism (1815)
topics.meaning: The Congress of Vienna of 1815 restored conservative monarchical order in Europe after Napoleon's defeat and aimed to undo revolutionary changes.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Following the defeat of Napoleon in 1815, European governments were driven by a spirit of conservatism that believed in preserving traditional institutions like the monarchy, the Church, social hierarchies, property and the family. Representatives of Britain, Russia, Prussia and Austria, who had collectively defeated Napoleon, met at the Congress of Vienna in 1815, hosted by the Austrian Chancellor Duke Metternich. The aim was to restore the monarchies overthrown by Napoleon and create a new conservative order in Europe. The Bourbon dynasty was restored to power in France, and France lost territories it had annexed under Napoleon. A series of states were set up on France's boundaries to prevent French expansion in future. Conservative regimes set up after 1815 were autocratic, did not tolerate criticism or dissent, and imposed censorship to curb ideas of liberty and freedom associated with the French Revolution.
topics.easyExplanation: After Napoleon was defeated in 1815, kings and conservatives met at Vienna to bring back the old order, restore monarchies and stop revolutionary ideas.

topics.topic: The Revolutions of 1830 and 1848
topics.meaning: The revolutionary uprisings of 1830 and 1848 spread liberal and nationalist ideas across Europe, challenging the conservative order.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: As conservative regimes tried to consolidate power, liberals and revolutionaries went underground, and the 1830s brought economic hardship across Europe. The first upheaval came in July 1830 when the Bourbon kings of France were overthrown by liberal revolutionaries who installed a constitutional monarchy under Louis Philippe; this July Revolution sparked an uprising in Brussels that led to Belgium breaking away from the United Kingdom of the Netherlands. In 1848 food shortages and widespread unemployment brought the population of Paris onto the roads, a republic was proclaimed, and the news inspired liberal middle classes in Germany, Italy, Poland and the Austro-Hungarian Empire to demand constitutions and national unification. In the German regions a large number of political associations came together in Frankfurt to vote for an all-German National Assembly at the Frankfurt Parliament, which met in the Church of St Paul and offered the crown to the Prussian king, who rejected it. Though most of these uprisings were eventually suppressed, the issues of liberty and the nation could not be reversed.
topics.easyExplanation: In 1830 and 1848 people across Europe rose up demanding freedom and nations; some failed but they kept the ideas of liberty and unity alive.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Economic problems in the 1830s pushed people toward revolt.

topics.topic: The Unification of Italy
topics.meaning: Italy was unified between 1859 and 1871 through the efforts of Giuseppe Mazzini, Count Cavour, Giuseppe Garibaldi and King Victor Emmanuel II.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: During the mid-nineteenth century Italy was divided into seven states, of which only Sardinia-Piedmont was ruled by an Italian princely house. Giuseppe Mazzini sought to forge a unified Italian Republic and founded the secret society Young Italy. After the failed revolutionary uprisings of 1831 and 1848, the responsibility of uniting the Italian states fell on Sardinia-Piedmont under its ruler King Victor Emmanuel II. Chief Minister Count Cavour, who led the movement, formed a tactful diplomatic alliance with France and defeated the Austrian forces in 1859. A large number of armed volunteers under Giuseppe Garibaldi joined the fray; in 1860 Garibaldi marched into South Italy and the Kingdom of the Two Sicilies and succeeded in winning the support of the local peasants in order to drive out the Spanish rulers. In 1861 Victor Emmanuel II was proclaimed king of united Italy, and with the addition of Rome in 1870, the unification was largely complete.
topics.easyExplanation: Italy was made one country between 1859 and 1871 by leaders like Mazzini, Cavour and Garibaldi, with Victor Emmanuel II becoming king in 1861.

topics.topic: The Unification of Germany
topics.meaning: Germany was unified in 1871 mainly through the policy of blood and iron pursued by Otto von Bismarck and the military strength of Prussia.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: After the liberal attempt to unite Germany at the Frankfurt Parliament in 1848 was repressed by the monarchy and aristocracy, Prussia took on the leadership of the movement for national unification. Its chief minister, Otto von Bismarck, was the architect of this process, carried out with the help of the Prussian army and bureaucracy and his policy of 'blood and iron'. Three wars over seven years, with Austria, Denmark and France, ended in Prussian victory and completed the process of unification. In January 1871 the Prussian king, William I, was proclaimed German Emperor in a ceremony held at the Palace of Versailles. The nation-building process in Germany demonstrated the dominance of Prussian state power, and the new German state placed strong emphasis on modernising the currency, banking, legal and judicial systems.
topics.easyExplanation: Germany became one nation in 1871 through Bismarck's policy of war and Prussian power; the Prussian king William I was made German Emperor at Versailles.

topics.topic: Britain and the Idea of Nationalism
topics.meaning: In Britain the nation-state was formed not through revolution but through a long process in which English dominance united England, Scotland, Wales and Ireland.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The model of the nation in Britain was the result of a long-drawn-out process rather than a sudden revolution. Before the eighteenth century there was no British nation; the primary identities of the people of the British Isles were ethnic, such as English, Welsh, Scot or Irish. The steady growth of English wealth and power enabled it to extend its influence over the other nations of the islands. The English Parliament, which had seized power from the monarchy in 1688, was the instrument through which a nation-state was formed. The Act of Union of 1707 between England and Scotland formed the United Kingdom of Great Britain, meaning that England could impose its influence on Scotland. Scotland's distinctive culture and political institutions were systematically suppressed, and the Catholic clans of the Scottish Highlands suffered repression. Ireland was forcibly incorporated into the United Kingdom in 1801 after a failed revolt led by Wolfe Tone. A new British nation was forged through the propagation of a dominant English culture, with symbols such as the British flag (Union Jack), the national anthem and the English language.
topics.easyExplanation: Britain became one nation slowly, as England gradually took over Scotland, Wales and Ireland, uniting them under English culture and symbols like the Union Jack.

topics.topic: Nationalism, Imperialism and the Balkans
topics.meaning: After 1871 nationalism turned narrow and aggressive, and the Balkans became a region of intense conflict that contributed to the outbreak of the First World War.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: After 1871 the major issue in European politics was the Balkans, a region of geographical and ethnic variation comprising modern-day Romania, Bulgaria, Albania, Greece, Macedonia, Croatia, Bosnia-Herzegovina, Slovenia, Serbia and Montenegro, whose inhabitants were broadly known as Slavs. A large part of the Balkans was under the control of the Ottoman Empire, which had built a powerful state but was now in decline. As different Slavic nationalities struggled to define their identity and independence, the Balkan area became an area of intense conflict. The Balkan states were fiercely jealous of each other and each hoped to gain more territory at the expense of others. Matters were further complicated because the Balkans also became the scene of big power rivalry among Russia, Germany, England and Austro-Hungary. This led to a series of wars in the region and finally the First World War in 1914. Meanwhile nationalist ideas were also used by European powers to justify imperialism, as each wished to extend its control over other areas.
topics.easyExplanation: After 1871 nationalism became aggressive, and the Balkans, full of competing nations and great power rivalry, became the trouble spot that helped trigger the First World War in 1914.

topics.topic: The First World War and Growth of Nationalism
topics.meaning: The First World War created new economic and political conditions in India that fuelled the growth of nationalism.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The First World War (1914-1918) created a new economic and political situation in India. It led to a huge increase in defence expenditure financed by war loans and increased taxes, while customs duties were raised and income tax was introduced. Through the war years prices increased, doubling between 1913 and 1918, which led to extreme hardship for the common people. Villages were called upon to supply soldiers, and forced recruitment in rural areas caused widespread anger. In 1918-19 and 1920-21 crops failed in many parts of India, resulting in acute shortages of food, while an influenza epidemic struck and according to the census of 1921 around 12 to 13 million people perished. People hoped that their hardships would end after the war, but that did not happen. It was in this context that a new leader, Mahatma Gandhi, emerged and suggested a novel method of struggle.
topics.easyExplanation: The First World War made life hard in India with high prices, taxes, forced recruitment, crop failure and disease, which increased anger against the British and helped nationalism grow.

stepByStepExplanation.title: War begins
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The First World War of 1914-1918 changed India's situation.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Economic burden

topics.topic: Satyagraha and Mahatma Gandhi's Early Campaigns
topics.meaning: Satyagraha was Mahatma Gandhi's method of non-violent struggle based on the power of truth, used first in Champaran, Kheda and Ahmedabad.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Mahatma Gandhi returned to India from South Africa in January 1915, where he had successfully fought the racist regime with a novel method of mass agitation called satyagraha. The idea of satyagraha emphasised the power of truth and the need to search for truth; it suggested that if the cause was true and the struggle was against injustice, then physical force was not necessary to fight the oppressor. A satyagrahi could win the battle through non-violence by appealing to the conscience of the oppressor. After arriving in India, Mahatma Gandhi successfully organised satyagraha movements in various places: in 1916 he travelled to Champaran in Bihar to inspire peasants to struggle against the oppressive indigo plantation system; in 1917 he organised a satyagraha to support the peasants of the Kheda district of Gujarat who could not pay revenue due to crop failure and a plague epidemic; and in 1918 he went to Ahmedabad to organise a satyagraha movement among cotton mill workers.
topics.easyExplanation: Satyagraha was Gandhi's way of fighting injustice with truth and non-violence; he first used it for peasants in Champaran and Kheda and mill workers in Ahmedabad.

topics.topic: The Rowlatt Act and Jallianwala Bagh
topics.meaning: The Rowlatt Act of 1919 gave the government repressive powers and provoked a nationwide satyagraha that led to the Jallianwala Bagh massacre.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Emboldened by his earlier successes, Gandhiji in 1919 decided to launch a nationwide satyagraha against the proposed Rowlatt Act of 1919. This Act had been hurriedly passed through the Imperial Legislative Council despite the united opposition of the Indian members; it gave the government enormous powers to repress political activities and allowed detention of political prisoners without trial for two years. Mahatma Gandhi called for a nationwide hartal on 6 April 1919, and rallies were organised in various cities. Alarmed by the popular upsurge, the British administration decided to clamp down on nationalists, and Mahatma Gandhi was barred from entering Delhi. On 13 April 1919 the infamous Jallianwala Bagh incident took place: a crowd gathered in the enclosed ground of Jallianwala Bagh in Amritsar, some to protest against the government's repressive measures and others to attend the annual Baisakhi fair. General Dyer entered the area, blocked the exit points and opened fire on the crowd, killing hundreds. As news of Jallianwala Bagh spread, crowds took to the streets in many north Indian towns.
topics.easyExplanation: The Rowlatt Act of 1919 allowed jailing people without trial; protests against it led to General Dyer killing hundreds at Jallianwala Bagh on 13 April 1919.

topics.topic: The Khilafat Movement and Non-Cooperation
topics.meaning: The Khilafat issue and the Non-Cooperation Movement, launched in 1920-21, brought Hindus and Muslims together in a mass struggle against British rule.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Mahatma Gandhi felt the need to launch a broad-based movement uniting Hindus and Muslims. One important issue was the Khilafat: the First World War had ended with the defeat of the Ottoman Empire, and rumours spread that a harsh peace treaty would be imposed on the Ottoman emperor, the spiritual head of the Islamic world or Khalifa. To defend his temporal powers a Khilafat Committee was formed in Bombay in March 1919, led by brothers Muhammad Ali and Shaukat Ali. At the Calcutta session of the Congress in September 1920 Gandhiji convinced other leaders to start a Non-Cooperation Movement in support of Khilafat and for swaraj. In his book Hind Swaraj (1909) Mahatma Gandhi had declared that British rule was established in India with the cooperation of Indians, and had survived only because of this cooperation. The Non-Cooperation Movement, adopted at the Nagpur session in December 1920, proposed surrendering titles, boycotting government schools, courts and councils, foreign goods, and if the government persisted, launching a full civil disobedience campaign.
topics.easyExplanation: The Khilafat issue and the Non-Cooperation Movement of 1920-21 united Hindus and Muslims to boycott British schools, courts and goods to win swaraj.

topics.topic: Non-Cooperation Movement in Towns and Countryside
topics.meaning: The Non-Cooperation Movement of 1921-22 spread to towns and the countryside, taking different forms among students, lawyers, peasants and tribals.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The Non-Cooperation Movement started in January 1921 and spread to different social groups, each interpreting swaraj in its own way. In the towns the movement began with the middle classes: thousands of students left government schools and colleges, headmasters and teachers resigned, and lawyers gave up their legal practices. The boycott of foreign cloth saw the import of foreign cloth halve between 1921 and 1922. In the countryside the struggle spread among peasants and tribals; in Awadh, peasants led by Baba Ramchandra struggled against talukdars and landlords who demanded high rents and a variety of other cesses. In the Gudem Hills of Andhra Pradesh, a militant guerrilla movement spread under the leadership of Alluri Sitaram Raju, who claimed special powers and asserted that India could be liberated only by the use of force. The movement, however, slowed down in the cities for various reasons, including the high cost of khadi cloth, the slow setting up of alternative institutions, and the difficulty of boycotting British institutions.
topics.easyExplanation: The Non-Cooperation Movement spread differently across India - students and lawyers boycotted institutions in towns, while peasants in Awadh and tribals in Gudem fought landlords and forest laws.

topics.topic: Chauri Chaura and the End of Non-Cooperation
topics.meaning: The Chauri Chaura incident of February 1922, in which a violent mob killed policemen, led Gandhi to call off the Non-Cooperation Movement.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In February 1922 Mahatma Gandhi decided to withdraw the Non-Cooperation Movement. He felt the movement was turning violent in many places and that satyagrahis needed to be properly trained before they would be ready for mass struggles. The immediate cause was the Chauri Chaura incident in the Gorakhpur district of the United Provinces, where a peaceful demonstration in a bazaar turned into a violent clash in which the crowd set fire to a police station, killing a number of policemen. Deeply disturbed by this violence, which went against the principle of non-violence at the heart of satyagraha, Gandhi called off the movement. Within the Congress, some leaders were tired of mass struggles and wanted to participate in elections to the provincial councils that had been set up by the Government of India Act of 1919. C.R. Das and Motilal Nehru formed the Swaraj Party to argue for a return to council politics, while younger leaders like Jawaharlal Nehru and Subhas Chandra Bose pressed for more radical mass agitation and full independence.
topics.easyExplanation: After a mob killed policemen at Chauri Chaura in February 1922, Gandhi stopped the Non-Cooperation Movement because it had turned violent.

topics.topic: Simon Commission and Purna Swaraj
topics.meaning: The Simon Commission's arrival in 1928 and the Lahore Congress of 1929 led the Congress to demand Purna Swaraj, or complete independence.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When the Tory government in Britain constituted a Statutory Commission under Sir John Simon to look into the functioning of the constitutional system in India and suggest changes, it had no Indian members and was seen as a denial of Indians' right to determine their own future. When the Simon Commission arrived in India in 1928 it was greeted with the slogan 'Go back Simon', and all parties, including the Congress and the Muslim League, participated in the demonstrations. In an effort to win them over, Viceroy Lord Irwin announced in October 1929 a vague offer of dominion status for India in an unspecified future and a Round Table Conference to discuss a future constitution. Under the presidency of Jawaharlal Nehru, the Lahore Congress of December 1929 formalised the demand of Purna Swaraj or full independence for India, and it was declared that 26 January 1930 would be celebrated as Independence Day, when people were to take a pledge to struggle for complete independence.
topics.easyExplanation: The all-British Simon Commission was protested with 'Go back Simon' in 1928, and at the Lahore Congress of 1929 the Congress demanded Purna Swaraj, declaring 26 January 1930 as Independence Day.

topics.topic: The Civil Disobedience Movement and the Salt March
topics.meaning: The Civil Disobedience Movement began in 1930 with Mahatma Gandhi's Salt March to Dandi, defying the British salt monopoly.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Mahatma Gandhi found in salt a powerful symbol that could unite the nation, since salt was consumed by rich and poor alike and was one of the most essential items of food. On 31 January 1930 Gandhi sent a letter to Viceroy Irwin stating eleven demands, the most stirring of which was the demand to abolish the salt tax. Mahatma Gandhi's letter was an ultimatum, and if the demands were not fulfilled by 11 March, the Congress would launch a civil disobedience campaign. When Irwin was unwilling to negotiate, Mahatma Gandhi started his famous Salt March accompanied by 78 of his trusted volunteers. The march was over 240 miles from Gandhi's ashram in Sabarmati to the Gujarati coastal town of Dandi. On 6 April 1930 Gandhi reached Dandi and ceremonially violated the law by manufacturing salt from seawater. This marked the beginning of the Civil Disobedience Movement, which differed from Non-Cooperation as people were now asked not only to refuse cooperation but to break colonial laws.
topics.easyExplanation: In 1930 Gandhi marched 240 miles to Dandi and made salt from seawater, breaking the salt law and starting the Civil Disobedience Movement where people openly broke British laws.

topics.topic: Participation of Different Groups and Limits of the Movement
topics.meaning: Various social groups including peasants, business classes, women and workers joined the Civil Disobedience Movement, but some groups stayed away.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The Civil Disobedience Movement drew in diverse social groups, each with its own aspirations. Rich peasant communities like the Patidars of Gujarat and the Jats of Uttar Pradesh, hit by falling cash crop prices, were active and saw swaraj as a struggle against high revenue. Poorer peasants wanted unpaid rents to the landlord to be remitted, but the Congress was unwilling to support them for fear of upsetting the rich peasants and landlords. The business classes, organised through bodies like the Indian Industrial and Commercial Congress (1920) and the Federation of the Indian Chamber of Commerce and Industries (FICCI, 1927), supported the movement and reacted against colonial policies that restricted their activities. Women participated in large numbers, joining protest marches, manufacturing salt and picketing shops, though the Congress was reluctant to allow them to hold positions of authority. Some groups stayed away: the industrial working class did not participate in large numbers except in the Nagpur region, and Dr B.R. Ambedkar, who organised the dalits, demanded separate electorates, leading to the Poona Pact of September 1932.
topics.easyExplanation: Rich peasants, business people and women joined the Civil Disobedience Movement, but poor peasants, most workers and dalits under Ambedkar largely stayed away or had different demands.

topics.topic: The Sense of Collective Belonging and National Symbols
topics.meaning: Nationalism spread through a sense of collective belonging created by symbols, songs, images and a reinterpreted history.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Nationalism spreads when people begin to believe that they are all part of the same nation, and a sense of collective belonging was created partly through the experience of united struggles and partly through cultural processes. The identity of the nation is most often symbolised in a figure or image: in the twentieth century, with the growth of nationalism, the identity of India came to be visually associated with the image of Bharat Mata. The image was first created by Bankim Chandra Chattopadhyay, who wrote the hymn 'Vande Mataram' as part of his novel Anandamath, inspiring devotion to the motherland. Abanindranath Tagore painted his famous image of Bharat Mata as a calm, composed, divine and spiritual figure. Indian folklore was revived, and in Bengal Rabindranath Tagore began collecting ballads, nursery rhymes and myths. During the Swadeshi movement a tricolour flag with red, green and yellow was designed in Bengal, and by 1921 Gandhi had designed the swaraj flag, with a spinning wheel in the centre symbolising self-help. Reinterpreting Indian history also helped Indians feel proud of their past and inspired them to struggle for the nation.
topics.easyExplanation: A feeling of belonging to one nation was built through symbols like Bharat Mata, songs like 'Vande Mataram', the swaraj flag and a proud retelling of Indian history.

chapters.chapter: The Making of a Global World

topics.topic: The Pre-Modern World and the Silk Routes
topics.meaning: The silk routes were ancient trade networks that connected distant parts of the world and carried goods, culture and religion.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The making of the global world has a long history of trade, migration, people in search of work, and movement of capital. The silk routes are a good example of vibrant pre-modern trade and cultural links between distant parts of the world. The name comes from the western-bound Chinese silk cargoes that travelled along these routes. Historians have identified several silk routes, overland and by sea, knitting together vast regions of Asia and linking Asia with Europe and northern Africa. They are known to have existed since before the Christian era and thrived almost till the fifteenth century. Chinese pottery travelled the same route, as did textiles and spices from India and Southeast Asia. In return precious metals like gold and silver flowed from Europe to Asia. Trade and cultural exchange always went hand in hand: early Christian missionaries almost certainly travelled this route to Asia, as did early Muslim preachers a few centuries later, and much before all of this, Buddhism emerged from eastern India and spread in several directions through the silk routes.
topics.easyExplanation: Long ago, the silk routes linked Asia, Europe and Africa, carrying silk, pottery, spices and precious metals as well as religions like Buddhism and Christianity.

topics.topic: Food Travels: Spaghetti and Potato
topics.meaning: The exchange of foods between distant regions shows how the world was being globally connected long before modern times.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Food offers many examples of long-distance cultural exchange. Traders and travellers introduced new crops to the lands they travelled. Even ready foodstuffs in distant parts of the world might share common origins; spaghetti and noodles are an example, as it is believed that noodles travelled west from China to become spaghetti, or that Arab traders took pasta to fifth-century Sicily. Many of our common foods such as potatoes, soya, groundnuts, maize, tomatoes, chillies and sweet potatoes were not known to our ancestors until about five centuries ago. These foods were introduced in Europe and Asia after Christopher Columbus accidentally discovered the vast continent that would later become known as the Americas. The humble potato transformed Europe; Ireland's poorest peasants became so dependent on potatoes that when disease destroyed the potato crop in the mid-1840s, hundreds of thousands died of starvation in the Great Irish Potato Famine.
topics.easyExplanation: Foods like noodles, potatoes and chillies travelled across the world, and the potato even changed European diets so much that its failure caused a famine in Ireland.

topics.topic: Conquest, Disease and the Americas
topics.meaning: European conquest of the Americas in the sixteenth century was aided by diseases like smallpox, against which native Americans had no immunity.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The Portuguese and Spanish conquest and colonisation of America was decisively under way by the mid-sixteenth century. America's vast lands, abundant crops and minerals began to transform trade and lives everywhere. Precious metals, particularly silver, from mines located in present-day Peru and Mexico enhanced Europe's wealth and financed its trade with Asia. The most powerful weapon of the Spanish conquerors was not a conventional military weapon at all; it was the germs such as those of smallpox that they carried on their person. Because America had been cut off from the rest of the world for millions of years, its original inhabitants had no immunity against these diseases that came from Europe. Smallpox in particular proved a deadly killer; once introduced, it spread deep into the continent ahead of any Europeans, killing and decimating whole communities and paving the way for conquest. Until the nineteenth century poverty and hunger were common in Europe, and thousands fled Europe for America, where until the nineteenth century plantations worked by slaves captured in Africa grew cotton and sugar for European markets.
topics.easyExplanation: Europeans conquered the Americas partly because diseases like smallpox killed native peoples who had no immunity, while American silver enriched Europe.

topics.topic: The Nineteenth Century: Flows of Trade, Labour and Capital
topics.meaning: In the nineteenth century the world economy was shaped by three flows or movements: of trade, of labour, and of capital.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Economists identify three types of movement or flows within international economic exchanges in the nineteenth century. The first is the flow of trade, which in the nineteenth century referred largely to trade in goods such as cloth or wheat. The second is the flow of labour, the migration of people in search of employment. The third is the movement of capital for short-term or long-term investments over long distances. All three flows were closely interwoven and affected peoples' lives more deeply now than ever before. A good example is the trade in food grains: as urban centres expanded and industry grew in Britain, the demand for food grains rose, pushing up prices. The Corn Laws restricted the import of corn into Britain, and under pressure from industrialists and urban dwellers these laws were abolished, after which food could be imported more cheaply than it could be produced within the country. As food prices fell, consumption in Britain rose, and faster industrial growth led to higher incomes and more food imports.
topics.easyExplanation: In the 1800s the world economy was connected through three flows - trade in goods, migration of labour, and movement of money for investment.

topics.topic: Rinderpest and the Colonisation of Africa
topics.meaning: Rinderpest, a cattle disease that struck Africa in the 1880s, destroyed African livelihoods and helped Europeans conquer and control the continent.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In the late nineteenth century Europeans were attracted to Africa due to its vast resources of land and minerals, intending to establish plantations and mines to produce crops and minerals for export to Europe. However, there was an unexpected problem: a shortage of labour willing to work for wages. In Africa land and livestock sustained African livelihoods, and people rarely worked for a wage. Employers used many methods to recruit and retain labour, including heavy taxes, changes in inheritance laws and confining miners to compounds. A striking example of the power of disease is the story of rinderpest, a fast-spreading cattle plague that hit Africa in the 1880s. It was carried by infected cattle imported from British Asia to feed the Italian soldiers invading Eritrea in East Africa. Entering Africa in the east, rinderpest moved west, reaching Africa's Atlantic coast in 1892 and the Cape (the southernmost tip) five years later in 1897. Along the way it killed about 90 per cent of the cattle. The loss of cattle destroyed African livelihoods, and planters, mine owners and colonial governments used this scarcity to force Africans into the labour market and strengthen their power.
topics.easyExplanation: Rinderpest killed most of Africa's cattle in the 1880s and 1890s, ruining African herders and forcing them to work for Europeans, which helped colonisation.

topics.topic: Indentured Labour Migration from India
topics.meaning: Indentured labour was a system of bonded migration in which Indian workers were sent on contracts to work on plantations and mines overseas in the nineteenth century.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In the nineteenth century hundreds of thousands of Indian and Chinese labourers went to work on plantations, in mines, and in road and railway construction projects around the world. In India indentured labourers were hired under contracts which promised return travel to India after they had worked five years on their employer's plantation. Most Indian indentured workers came from the present-day regions of eastern Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, central India and the dry districts of Tamil Nadu, areas where cottage industries declined, land rents rose, and lands were cleared for mines and plantations, all of which affected the lives of the poor. The main destinations of Indian indentured migrants were the Caribbean islands (mainly Trinidad, Guyana and Surinam), Mauritius and Fiji, while closer home Tamil migrants went to Ceylon and Malaya, and many went to Assam tea plantations. Recruitment was done by agents who often provided false information about final destinations, modes of travel, the nature of the work and living conditions. Living and working conditions were harsh, and workers discovered ways to survive; some described the experience as a new system of slavery.
topics.easyExplanation: Indian indentured labourers signed contracts to work for five years on overseas plantations in places like the Caribbean, Mauritius and Fiji, often deceived and treated almost like slaves.

topics.topic: The Inter-War Economy and the Great Depression
topics.meaning: The First World War and the Great Depression of 1929 brought severe economic crises that disrupted the global economy in the inter-war period.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The First World War (1914-1918) was a war like no other before, the first modern industrial war fought with machine guns, tanks, aircraft and chemical weapons on a massive scale, and it transformed the world economically and politically. The war led to the snapping of economic links between some of the world's largest economic powers, which were now fighting each other. Britain borrowed large sums of money from US banks, and the war thus transformed the US from being an international debtor to an international creditor. The most important feature of the inter-war years was the Great Depression, which began around 1929 and lasted till the mid-1930s, during which most parts of the world experienced catastrophic declines in production, employment, incomes and trade. Agricultural overproduction remained a problem, made worse by falling agricultural prices; as prices slumped and agricultural incomes declined, farmers tried to expand production and bring even larger volumes of produce to the market to maintain their incomes, which pushed prices down further. In the US thousands of banks went bankrupt and were forced to close, and the Depression affected India's exports and imports, which nearly halved between 1928 and 1934.
topics.easyExplanation: The First World War and the Great Depression of 1929 caused worldwide collapse in production, jobs and trade, hitting farmers, banks and countries like India hard.

topics.topic: Rebuilding the World Economy after the Second World War
topics.meaning: After the Second World War the Bretton Woods system was set up to preserve economic stability and full employment in the industrial world.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The Second World War (1939-1945) was fought between the Axis powers (mainly Germany, Japan and Italy) and the Allies (Britain, France, the Soviet Union and the US), and it caused immense destruction, with at least 60 million people, or about 3 per cent of the world's 1939 population, killed. Two crucial influences shaped post-war reconstruction: the US's emergence as the dominant economic, political and military power, and the dominance of the Soviet Union. The main aim of the post-war international economic system was to preserve economic stability and full employment in the industrial world. Its framework was agreed upon at the United Nations Monetary and Financial Conference held in July 1944 at Bretton Woods in New Hampshire, USA. The Bretton Woods conference established the International Monetary Fund (IMF) to deal with external surpluses and deficits of its member nations, and the International Bank for Reconstruction and Development, popularly known as the World Bank, was set up to finance post-war reconstruction. The post-war international economic system is also often described as the Bretton Woods system.
topics.easyExplanation: After the Second World War, the Bretton Woods conference of 1944 created the IMF and the World Bank to keep the world economy stable and rebuild war-damaged countries.

topics.topic: Decolonisation and the End of Bretton Woods
topics.meaning: The decline of the Bretton Woods system and the rise of newly independent nations transformed the global economy from the 1960s onwards.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The Bretton Woods institutions, the IMF and the World Bank, are also known as the Bretton Woods twins, and the post-war international economic system based on them inaugurated an era of unprecedented growth of trade and incomes for the Western industrial nations and Japan. But from the 1960s the rising costs of its overseas involvements weakened the US's finances and competitive strength, and the US dollar no longer commanded confidence as the world's principal currency; it could not maintain its value in relation to gold. This eventually led to the collapse of the system of fixed exchange rates and the introduction of a system of floating exchange rates in the early 1970s. Meanwhile, most developing countries did not benefit from the fast growth the Western economies experienced, so they organised themselves as a group, the Group of 77 (G-77), to demand a New International Economic Order (NIEO), a system that would give them real control over their natural resources, more development assistance, fairer prices for raw materials, and better access for their manufactured goods in developed countries' markets.
topics.easyExplanation: From the 1960s the Bretton Woods system weakened, fixed exchange rates collapsed in the 1970s, and poorer nations formed the G-77 to demand a fairer global economy.

topics.topic: The Great Depression and India
topics.meaning: The Great Depression had a severe impact on India, especially on peasants and farmers, deepening rural distress in the early 1930s.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: India was crucial to the late nineteenth-century world economy, but the Great Depression immediately affected Indian trade, as India's exports and imports nearly halved between 1928 and 1934. As international prices crashed, prices in India also plunged: between 1928 and 1934 wheat prices in India fell by 50 per cent. Peasants and farmers suffered more than urban dwellers, for though agricultural prices fell sharply the colonial government refused to reduce revenue demands, and their burden of debt increased. Peasants used up their savings, mortgaged lands, and sold whatever jewellery and precious metals they had to meet their expenses. In these depression years India became an exporter of precious metals, notably gold, and the famished and impoverished peasants of India produced and exported gold that helped speed up Britain's recovery, though it did little to help Indian peasants. The Depression proved less grim for urban India, where falling prices helped those with fixed incomes; nevertheless it created sharp discontent among peasants which fed into nationalist movements, and it was in this very period that the Civil Disobedience Movement was launched.
topics.easyExplanation: The Great Depression hit Indian peasants hard as crop prices crashed while revenue demands stayed high, forcing them to sell gold which helped Britain recover, not India.

topics.topic: Proto-Industrialisation
topics.meaning: Proto-industrialisation was the phase of large-scale industrial production for an international market before factories began in England and Europe.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Even before factories began to dot the landscape in England and Europe, there was large-scale industrial production for an international market, a phase historians refer to as proto-industrialisation. In this period merchants from the towns in Europe began moving to the countryside, supplying money to peasants and artisans and persuading them to produce for an international market. This was due to the expansion of world trade and the acquisition of colonies in different parts of the world, which increased the demand for goods. Merchants could not expand production within towns because urban crafts and trade guilds were powerful associations that controlled production, regulated competition and prices, and restricted the entry of new people into the trade. In the countryside, poor peasants and artisans who had earlier depended on common lands now looked for alternative sources of income and willingly took to producing for the merchants. Within this system a close relationship developed between the town and the countryside: merchants were based in towns, but the work was done mostly in the countryside, and a finished product passed through several hands before reaching the merchant and the export market.

topics.topic: The Coming of the Factory
topics.meaning: The earliest factories in England came up in the 1730s and multiplied in the late eighteenth century, especially in the cotton industry.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The earliest factories in England came up by the 1730s, but it was only in the late eighteenth century that the number of factories multiplied. Cotton was the first symbol of the new era; its production boomed in the late nineteenth century. In 1760 Britain was importing 2.5 million pounds of raw cotton to feed its cotton industry, and by 1787 this import soared to 22 million pounds. A series of inventions in the eighteenth century increased the efficiency of each step of the production process, such as carding, twisting, spinning and rolling, and enhanced the output per worker, enabling each worker to produce more, and they also produced stronger threads and yarn. Richard Arkwright created the cotton mill, where costly new machines could be set up, all the processes could be brought together under one roof and management, and a more careful supervision over the production process and a watch over quality and the regulation of labour became possible. Until this time such control had not been possible in the countryside.
topics.easyExplanation: Factories first appeared in England in the 1730s and grew rapidly later in the cotton industry, where new machines and Arkwright's mill brought all work under one roof.

topics.topic: Pace of Industrial Change
topics.meaning: The pace of industrial change in Britain was uneven, with traditional industries surviving alongside the new cotton and metal industries.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The pace of industrial change in Britain did not happen as dramatically as is often imagined. The most dynamic industries in Britain were clearly cotton and metals; cotton was the leading sector in the first phase of industrialisation up to the 1840s, after which the iron and steel industry led the way, especially with the expansion of railways in England from the 1840s and in the colonies from the 1860s. Yet the new industries could not easily displace traditional industries: even at the end of the nineteenth century less than 20 per cent of the total workforce was employed in technologically advanced industrial sectors, and textiles was a dynamic sector but a large portion of the output was produced not within factories but outside, within domestic units. Many industries grew through a series of small innovations, and ordinary and small innovations were the basis of growth in many non-mechanised sectors such as food processing, building, pottery, glass work, tanning, furniture making and production of implements. Technological changes occurred slowly because new technology was expensive, and machines often broke down and repair was costly.
topics.easyExplanation: Industrialisation in Britain was slow and uneven; cotton and metals led but old hand industries survived, and most workers were not in mechanised factories even by 1900.

topics.topic: Hand Labour and Steam Power
topics.meaning: In Victorian Britain there was no shortage of human labour, so industrialists often preferred hand labour over expensive machines.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In Victorian Britain there was no shortage of human labour, and poor peasants and vagrants moved to the cities in large numbers in search of jobs, waiting for work. When there is plenty of labour, wages are low, so industrialists had no problem of labour shortage or high wage costs and did not want to introduce machines that got rid of human labour and required large capital investment. In many industries the demand for labour was seasonal, as in gas works and breweries, bookbinding and printing, and these required workers only at certain times, so industrialists preferred hand labour, employing workers for the season. A range of products could be produced only with hand labour, and intricate designs and specific shapes could be made by hand; in Victorian Britain the upper classes, the aristocrats and the bourgeoisie, preferred things produced by hand because handmade products came to symbolise refinement and class. Machine-made goods were for export to the colonies. In countries with labour shortage, such as nineteenth-century America, industrialists were keen to use mechanical power so that the need for human labour could be minimised.
topics.easyExplanation: Because Britain had plenty of cheap workers, factory owners often chose hand labour over machines, and handmade goods were valued by the rich for their quality.

topics.topic: Life of Workers in Industrial Britain
topics.meaning: Workers in industrial Britain faced abundant job seekers, low wages, unemployment, poor housing and harsh living conditions.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The abundance of labour in the market affected the lives of workers; as news of possible jobs travelled to the countryside, hundreds tramped to the cities. The actual possibility of getting a job depended on existing networks of friendship and kin relations, and many job seekers had to wait weeks, spending nights under bridges or in night shelters. Seasonality of work in many industries meant prolonged periods without work, and after the busy season was over the poor were on the streets again. Some returned to the countryside, but most looked for odd jobs, which until the mid-nineteenth century were difficult to find. Wages increased somewhat in the early nineteenth century, but the increase in wages meant little when prices rose, and during the Napoleonic Wars the real value of what workers earned fell. The fear of unemployment made workers hostile to the introduction of new technology; when the Spinning Jenny was introduced in the woollen industry, women who survived on hand spinning began attacking the new machines. After the 1840s, building activity intensified in the cities, and the new opportunities for work increased employment.
topics.easyExplanation: Life for British workers was hard - too many job seekers, low wages, unemployment between seasons, and poor housing, and they often feared new machines like the Spinning Jenny would take their jobs.

topics.topic: Industrialisation in the Colonies: Indian Textiles
topics.meaning: Before the age of machine industries, India dominated the world export market for cotton and silk textiles.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Before the age of machine industries, silk and cotton goods from India dominated the international market in textiles. Coarser cottons were produced in many countries, but the finer varieties often came from India. Armenian and Persian merchants took goods from Punjab to Afghanistan, eastern Persia and Central Asia. A vibrant sea trade operated through the main pre-colonial ports: Surat on the Gujarat coast connected India to the Gulf and Red Sea Ports; Masulipatam on the Coromandel coast and Hoogly in Bengal had trade links with Southeast Asian ports. A variety of Indian merchants and bankers were involved in this network of export trade, financing production, carrying goods and supplying exporters. By the 1750s this network, controlled by Indian merchants, was breaking down. The European companies gradually gained power, first securing a variety of concessions from local courts, then the monopoly rights to trade. This resulted in a decline of the old ports of Surat and Hoogly through which local merchants had operated, and exports from these ports fell dramatically while Bombay and Calcutta grew as the new ports under colonial control.
topics.easyExplanation: Before machine factories, Indian cotton and silk textiles led the world market, but European companies gradually took control of the trade and the old Indian ports declined.

topics.topic: Decline of Indian Weavers under Colonial Rule
topics.meaning: Indian weavers and the textile industry declined in the nineteenth century due to Manchester imports, loss of markets and shortage of raw cotton.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Once the East India Company established political power, it asserted a monopoly right to trade and developed a system of management and control that eliminated competition, controlled costs, and ensured regular supplies of cotton and silk goods through paid servants called the gomasthas. The Company prevented weavers from dealing with other buyers through a system of advances: weavers who took loans were obliged to sell their produce to the gomastha and could not take their goods to other traders. In the nineteenth century Indian weavers faced two problems at the same time: their export market collapsed, and the local market shrank, being glutted with Manchester imports. Cotton manufactures from Britain flooded Indian markets, and by the 1850s reports from most weaving regions narrated stories of decline and devastation. By the 1860s weavers faced a new problem, as they could not get sufficient supply of raw cotton of good quality. When the American Civil War broke out and cotton supplies from the US were cut off, Britain turned to India, and raw cotton exports from India increased, pushing up prices, so Indian weavers were starved of supplies and forced to buy raw cotton at exorbitant prices.
topics.easyExplanation: Indian weavers declined in the 1800s as cheap Manchester cloth flooded markets, exports collapsed, and raw cotton became scarce and costly after the American Civil War.

topics.topic: Factories Come Up in India
topics.meaning: Indian factories, especially cotton mills in Bombay and jute mills in Bengal, came up from the 1850s onwards, set up by Indian and European entrepreneurs.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The first cotton mill in Bombay came up in 1854, and it went into production two years later in 1856. By 1862 four mills were at work with 94,000 spindles and 2,150 looms. Around the same time jute mills came up in Bengal, the first being set up in 1855 and another seven years later in 1862. In the 1860s the Elgin Mill was started in Kanpur, and in the following year the first cotton mill of Ahmedabad was set up; by 1874 the first spinning and weaving mill of Madras began production. Many of the early entrepreneurs were merchants and bankers; in Bengal, Dwarkanath Tagore made his fortune in the China trade before he turned to industrial investment, while in Bombay Parsis like Dinshaw Petit and Jamsetjee Nusserwanjee Tata built huge industrial empires in India. Capital was accumulated through other trade networks, and some merchants from Madras traded with Burma while others had links with the Middle East and East Africa. The trade with China that developed from the late eighteenth century also generated profits, much of which was invested in setting up factories.
topics.easyExplanation: From the 1850s Indian factories like Bombay cotton mills and Bengal jute mills came up, founded by Indian merchants and businessmen such as the Tatas and Dwarkanath Tagore.

topics.topic: The Peculiarities of Industrial Growth and Markets
topics.meaning: Indian industrial growth was shaped by colonial policy, with European managing agencies controlling much of industry and small-scale production also expanding.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: European Managing Agencies, which were interested in certain kinds of products, controlled a large sector of Indian industries. They established tea and coffee plantations, acquiring land at cheap rates from the colonial government, and they invested in mining, indigo and jute, products primarily for export trade and not for sale in India. When Indian businessmen began setting up industries in the late nineteenth century, they avoided competing with Manchester goods in the Indian market, so Indian industrialists produced coarse cotton yarn rather than fabric, and the yarn produced in Indian spinning mills was used by handloom weavers in India or exported to China. By the first decade of the twentieth century a series of changes affected the pattern of industrialisation: the swadeshi movement gave a boost to local industry, industrial groups pressured the government to increase tariff protection, and as nationalist opinion grew, the colonial government was forced to grant some concessions. During the First World War, with British mills busy supplying war needs, Manchester imports into India declined, and Indian mills had a vast home market to supply, so over the war years industrial production boomed.
topics.easyExplanation: Indian industry was dominated by European managing agencies for export goods, while Indian mills made coarse yarn; the swadeshi movement and the First World War later helped Indian industry grow.

topics.topic: Small-Scale Industries and the Market for Goods
topics.meaning: Small-scale and handloom production persisted and even grew in colonial India, while advertisements and labels were used to create markets for goods.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Factory industries grew slowly, and large industries formed only a small segment of the economy; most of them, about 67 per cent in 1911, were located in Bengal and Bombay, while in the rest of the country small-scale production continued to predominate. Only a small proportion of the total industrial labour force worked in registered factories, and the rest worked in small workshops and household units, often located in alleys and bylanes invisible to the passer-by. Handicrafts production actually expanded in the twentieth century, and handloom cloth production expanded steadily, almost trebling between 1900 and 1940. This happened because of technological changes: handicrafts people adopt new technology that helps them improve production without excessively pushing up costs, so by the second decade of the twentieth century weavers used looms with a fly shuttle, which increased productivity, speeded up production and reduced labour demand. To sell their products, manufacturers used advertisements and labels; Manchester industrialists put labels on cloth bundles with the words 'MADE IN MANCHESTER', and images of Indian gods and important personages were used to make the products appear familiar and to give the manufactured goods a sense of authority.

chapters.chapter: Print Culture and the Modern World

topics.topic: The First Printed Books in East Asia
topics.meaning: The earliest print technology developed in China, Japan and Korea as a system of hand printing using woodblocks.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The earliest kind of print technology was developed in China, Japan and Korea, where it was a system of hand printing. From AD 594 onwards, books in China were printed by rubbing paper, also invented there, against the inked surface of woodblocks. As both sides of the thin, porous sheet could not be printed, the traditional Chinese accordion book was folded and stitched at the side. Skilled craftsmen could duplicate, with remarkable accuracy, the beauty of calligraphy. For a very long time the imperial state in China was the major producer of printed material, as China possessed a huge bureaucratic system that recruited its personnel through civil service examinations, and textbooks for these examinations were printed in vast numbers under the sponsorship of the imperial state. From the sixteenth century the number of examination candidates went up, and that increased the volume of print. The diversification of uses of print led to its expansion, and by the seventeenth century, as urban culture bloomed in China, print was no longer used just by scholar-officials, but merchants used print in their everyday life, and reading became a leisure activity.

topics.topic: Print Comes to Japan and Europe
topics.meaning: Buddhist missionaries introduced hand printing to Japan, and Marco Polo brought knowledge of woodblock printing from China to Europe.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Buddhist missionaries from China introduced hand-printing technology into Japan around AD 768-770. The oldest Japanese book, printed in AD 868, is the Buddhist Diamond Sutra, containing six sheets of text and woodcut illustrations. Pictures were printed on textiles, playing cards and paper money, and in medieval Japan poets and prose writers were regularly published, so books were cheap and abundant. In the late eighteenth century, in the flourishing urban circles at Edo (later to be known as Tokyo), illustrated collections of paintings depicted an elegant urban culture. Meanwhile in Europe, for centuries silk and spices from China flowed into Europe through the silk route, and in the eleventh century Chinese paper reached Europe via the same route. In 1295 Marco Polo, a great explorer, returned to Italy after many years of exploration in China, bringing with him the knowledge of woodblock printing. Now Italians began producing books with woodblocks, and soon the technology spread to other parts of Europe.

topics.topic: Gutenberg and the Printing Press
topics.meaning: Johann Gutenberg developed the first printing press in Europe around 1448, and the Gutenberg Bible was the first printed book.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Johann Gutenberg was the son of a merchant and grew up on a large agricultural estate. From his childhood he had seen wine and olive presses, and subsequently he learnt the art of polishing stones, became a master goldsmith, and acquired the expertise to create lead moulds used for making trinkets. Drawing on this knowledge, Gutenberg adapted existing technology to design his innovation. The olive press provided the model for the printing press, and moulds were used for casting the metal types for the letters of the alphabet. By 1448 Gutenberg perfected the system, and the first book he printed was the Bible. About 180 copies were printed, and it took three years to produce them, which was fast by the standards of those times. The new technology did not entirely displace the existing art of producing books by hand; printed books at first closely resembled written manuscripts in appearance and layout. The metal letters imitated the ornamental handwritten styles, borders were illuminated by hand, and illustrations were painted.
topics.easyExplanation: Around 1448 Johann Gutenberg invented Europe's first printing press using movable metal type, and printed the Gutenberg Bible, about 180 copies in three years.

topics.topic: The Print Revolution and Its Impact
topics.meaning: The print revolution transformed Europe by lowering the cost of books, creating a reading public and changing the relationship of people with knowledge.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The shift from hand printing to mechanical printing led to the print revolution. Printing presses were set up in most countries of Europe over the next 150 years after Gutenberg, and the second half of the fifteenth century saw 20 million copies of printed books flooding the markets in Europe; the number went up in the sixteenth century to about 200 million copies. This shift from hand printing to mechanical printing transformed the lives of people, changing their relationship to information and knowledge, and with institutions and authorities. Printing reduced the cost of books, as the time and labour required to produce each book came down, and multiple copies could be produced with greater ease. Books flooded the market, reaching an ever-growing readership, and access to books created a new culture of reading. Earlier reading had been restricted to the elites, and common people lived in a world of oral culture, but now a transition took place from oral culture to a reading culture, although the line between the two was not very sharp.
topics.easyExplanation: After Gutenberg, printing spread fast and made books cheap and plentiful, creating a new reading public and changing how people related to knowledge and authority.

topics.topic: Print and the Reformation
topics.meaning: Print played a key role in the Protestant Reformation, as Martin Luther's writings spread rapidly and challenged the authority of the Catholic Church.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Print and popular religious literature stimulated many distinctive individual interpretations of faith even among little-educated working people. In 1517 the religious reformer Martin Luther wrote Ninety Five Theses criticising many of the practices and rituals of the Roman Catholic Church. A printed copy of this was posted on a church door in Wittenberg, and it challenged the Church to debate his ideas. Luther's writings were immediately reproduced in vast numbers and read widely. This led to a division within the Church and to the beginning of the Protestant Reformation. Luther's translation of the New Testament sold 5,000 copies within a few weeks, and a second edition appeared within three months. Deeply grateful to print, Luther said, 'Printing is the ultimate gift of God and the greatest one.' Several scholars, in fact, think that print brought about a new intellectual atmosphere and helped spread the new ideas that led to the Reformation.
topics.easyExplanation: Printing spread Martin Luther's Ninety Five Theses of 1517 quickly, helping start the Protestant Reformation and challenge the Catholic Church.

topics.topic: Print, Censorship and Fear of Print
topics.meaning: The spread of print worried religious and political authorities, who feared rebellious ideas and imposed censorship through measures like the Index of Prohibited Books.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Not everyone welcomed the printed book, and even those who did also fearfully thought about the consequences of the spread of books. Many were apprehensive of the effects that the easier access to the printed word and the wider circulation of books could have on people's minds; it was feared that if there was no control over what was printed and read, then rebellious and irreligious thoughts might spread. If that happened, the authority of valuable literature would be destroyed. The Roman Church, troubled by such effects of popular readings and questionings of faith, imposed severe controls over publishers and booksellers and began to maintain an Index of Prohibited Books from 1558. The story of a sixteenth-century miller named Menocchio illustrates this fear: he began to read books available in his locality, reinterpreted the message of the Bible, and formulated a view of God and Creation that enraged the Roman Catholic Church. When the Roman Church began its inquisition to repress heretical ideas, Menocchio was hauled up twice and ultimately executed. Apprehension and fear continued, and authorities tried to control print.
topics.easyExplanation: Religious and political authorities feared that printed books would spread rebellious ideas, so the Catholic Church kept an Index of Prohibited Books from 1558 and punished free thinkers like Menocchio.

topics.topic: Print and the French Revolution
topics.meaning: Print is argued to have created conditions for the French Revolution by spreading enlightenment ideas, debate and criticism of authority.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Many historians have argued that print culture created the conditions within which the French Revolution occurred. Three types of arguments are usually put forward. First, print popularised the ideas of the Enlightenment thinkers; collectively their writings provided a critical commentary on tradition, superstition and despotism, and they argued for the rule of reason rather than custom, and demanded that everything be judged through the application of reason and rationality. The writings of Voltaire and Rousseau were read widely, and those who read these books saw the world through new eyes. Second, print created a new culture of dialogue and debate, as all values, norms and institutions were re-evaluated and discussed by a public that had become aware of the power of reason. Third, by the 1780s there was an outpouring of literature that mocked the royalty and criticised their morality, and this literature circulated underground and led to the growth of hostile sentiments against the monarchy. However, people did not read just one kind of literature, and they accepted some ideas while ignoring others, so the relationship between print and revolution was not a simple one.
topics.easyExplanation: Many historians believe print helped cause the French Revolution by spreading Enlightenment ideas, encouraging debate, and mocking the monarchy.

topics.topic: Print Comes to India
topics.meaning: Print was brought to India by Portuguese missionaries in Goa in the mid-sixteenth century, and Indian-language printing developed over the following centuries.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The printing press first came to Goa with Portuguese missionaries in the mid-sixteenth century. Jesuit priests learnt Konkani and printed several tracts. By 1674 about 50 books had been printed in the Konkani and Kanara languages. Catholic priests printed the first Tamil book in 1579 at Cochin, and in 1713 the first Malayalam book was printed by them. By 1710 Dutch Protestant missionaries had printed 32 Tamil texts, many of them translations of older works. The English language press did not grow in India until quite late, even though the English East India Company began to import presses from the late seventeenth century. From 1780 James Augustus Hickey began to edit the Bengal Gazette, a weekly magazine that described itself as 'a commercial paper open to all, but influenced by none'. By the close of the eighteenth century a number of newspapers and journals appeared in print, and there were Indians, too, who began to publish Indian newspapers. The first to appear was the weekly Bengal Gazette, brought out by Gangadhar Bhattacharya, who was close to Rammohun Roy.
topics.easyExplanation: Print came to India through Portuguese missionaries in Goa in the 1500s, and the first newspapers, like Hickey's Bengal Gazette of 1780, appeared later.

topics.topic: Print, Reform and Public Debate in India
topics.meaning: From the early nineteenth century print became a battleground of ideas in India, used for religious and social reform and intense public debate.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: From the early nineteenth century there were intense debates around religious issues, and different groups confronted the changes happening within colonial society in different ways and offered a variety of new interpretations of the beliefs of different religions. Some criticised existing practices and campaigned for reform, while others countered the arguments of reformers. These debates were carried out in public and in print. Printed tracts and newspapers not only spread the new ideas but shaped the nature of the debate, and a wider public could now participate in these public discussions and express their views. New ideas emerged through these clashes of opinions. Rammohun Roy published the Sambad Kaumudi from 1821, and the Hindu orthodoxy commissioned the Samachar Chandrika to oppose his opinions. From 1822 two Persian newspapers were published, the Jam-i-Jahan Nama and the Shamsul Akhbar, and in the same year a Gujarati newspaper, the Bombay Samachar, made its appearance. Among Muslims, the ulama feared that colonial rulers would encourage conversion, and they used cheap lithographic presses, published Persian and Urdu translations of holy scriptures, and printed religious newspapers and tracts.
topics.easyExplanation: In nineteenth-century India, print became a tool for social and religious reform and public debate, with reformers like Rammohun Roy and their opponents arguing through newspapers and tracts.

topics.topic: Print, Women, the Poor and Colonial Censorship
topics.meaning: Print expanded among women and the poor in nineteenth-century India and provoked debates, while the colonial government tightened censorship.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Print culture had a deep impact on the lives of women, the poor and on attitudes to caste. Lives and feelings of women began to be written in particularly vivid and intense ways; women's reading increased enormously in middle-class homes, and liberal husbands and fathers began educating their womenfolk at home and sent them to schools. Many journals began carrying writings by women and explained why women should be educated, while conservatives feared educated women would be widowed. A woman named Rashsundari Debi wrote her autobiography Amar Jiban, the first full-length autobiography published in the Bengali language. Very cheap small books were brought to markets in nineteenth-century Madras towns and sold at crossroads, allowing poor people travelling to markets to buy them. From the 1860s a few Bengali periodicals and Jyotiba Phule, the Maratha pioneer of low-caste protest movements, wrote about the injustices of the caste system. Initially the colonial state was not too concerned with censorship, but after the Revolt of 1857 the attitude changed, and the Vernacular Press Act of 1878 was passed, modelled on the Irish Press Laws, giving the government extensive rights to censor reports and editorials in the vernacular press.
topics.easyExplanation: Print reached women and the poor in nineteenth-century India, encouraging women's writing and low-caste protest, while after 1857 the British passed the Vernacular Press Act of 1878 to censor Indian newspapers.
